coredata.dc:description: The creation of smart, real-time sensing devices is crucial in food monitoring, though it continues to be a major challenge. Here, we report an eco-friendly fluorescent detection method for Cu2+ ions using 2-aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-H2BDC). Fluorescence (FL) quenching occurs at 425 nm under excitation at 330 nm, with detection limits (LOD) as low as 16.25 nM for Cu2+ ions. Additionally, a smartphone can visually and quantitatively detect Cu2+ ions by analyzing the RGB values on test paper. The results show high sensitivity, with a LOD of 11.07 μM. This work not only reveals fluorescent detection methods but also develops a convenient, low-cost, and portable smartphone-integrated paper strip for Cu2+ ion sensing. Two metrics, AGREEprep and BAGI, were employed to assess the method's environmental friendliness. The evaluation confirmed its exceptional eco-friendliness. This method marks a significant advancement for Cu2+ ion monitoring in diverse fields, especially food monitoring.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly fluorescent detection method for Cu2+ ions combined with smartphone-integrated paper strip sensors based on highly fluorescent 2-aminoterephthalic acid in milk samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The creation of smart, real-time sensing devices is crucial in food monitoring, though it continues to be a major challenge. Here, we report an eco-friendly fluorescent detection method for Cu2+ ions using 2-aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-H2BDC). Fluorescence (FL) quenching occurs at 425 nm under excitation at 330 nm, with detection limits (LOD) as low as 16.25 nM for Cu2+ ions. Additionally, a smartphone can visually and quantitatively detect Cu2+ ions by analyzing the RGB values on test paper. The results show high sensitivity, with a LOD of 11.07 μM. This work not only reveals fluorescent detection methods but also develops a convenient, low-cost, and portable smartphone-integrated paper strip for Cu2+ ion sensing. Two metrics, AGREEprep and BAGI, were employed to assess the method's environmental friendliness. The evaluation confirmed its exceptional eco-friendliness. This method marks a significant advancement for Cu2+ ion monitoring in diverse fields, especially food monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion (Cu2+), alongside other elements, emerges as a crucial determinant impacting human health (Sannok et al., 2023; Cao et al., 2019; Li et al., 2024; Zheng et al., 2024). It has the potential to infiltrate water sources or the food chain, resulting in significant repercussions. While essential for various bodily functions, excessive intake of copper has been associated with adverse outcomes. Specifically, it can prompt accelerated protein misfolding and aggregation, leading to the development of amyloid fibrils, a process closely linked to the pathogenesis of Parkinson’s disease (Guo et al., 2015; Viles and John, 2012; Xu et al., 2023). Copper is universally acknowledged as a fundamental trace element indispensable for the functioning of all living organisms, playing a substantial role in physiological and biological processes (Vopálenská et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2016). Despite the human body's innate mechanisms for regulating copper levels, exceeding the permissible threshold can trigger negative effects such as vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, nausea, and a metallic taste in the mouth (Emami and Mousazadeh, 2021). Furthermore, excessive accumulation of copper can inflict severe harm on organs such as the liver, brain, kidneys, and other tissues, thereby increasing health risks (Kaur and Verma, 2018). To tackle these issues, it is essential to develop a highly sensitive and selective method for detecting trace levels of Cu2+.

The methods for detecting Cu2+ generally include Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS) (De Oliveira (Souza et al., 2015), Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry (ICPMS) (Kayani and Abdullah, 2024; Kayani et al., 2023; Martín-Cameán et al., 2014) Chromatography (Kayani and Omer, 2022; Shatery et al., 2024; Bakkaus et al., 2006), chemiluminesce (Cheng et al., 2012), the electrochemical (Meng et al., 2014) have been used as for this purpose. However, each of these methods possesses its own limitations. These may include high costs or time-consuming procedures, the necessity of sophisticated equipment or laborious sample preparation, as well as the involvement of hazardous chemicals, including toxic precursor materials, in the analytical process (Arputharaj et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2024). On the contrary, fluorescent sensing probes offer various advantages, including straightforward preparation, affordability, convenient handling, rapid result acquisition, and superior sensitivity and selectivity (Mustafa et al., 2024; Kayani et al., 2024; Kayani et al., 2024; Kayani et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2009; Mu et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2024). Therefore, FL chemosensors for Cu2+ detection are currently being actively developed (Goswami et al., 2024). Generally, the method involves complexation of the ligand with Cu2+, utilizing a ligand that includes an electron donor atom like oxygen or nitrogen (Nam et al., 2023; Kim et al., 2020).

Several fluorophores have been reported for detecting the presence of Cu2+, such as quinoline, dansyl amide, naphthalimide, fluorescein, rhodamine, and pyrazine. However, some disadvantages have been noted, including low FL quantum yield, low stability, and challenging synthetic pathways (Srisuwan et al., 2022). One particularly interesting fluorophore molecule is 2-aminoterephthalic acid, due to its good photostability, and high FL quantum yields. It offers efficacy as a Cu2+ probe, serving as a reaction site for sensing. Thus, the concept of using NH2-H2BDC acid as a recognition and chelating unit in a newly designed chemosensor for Cu2+ detection has emerged.

On the other hand, the advancement of mobile technology has opened up opportunities to maximize the benefits of fluorescent chemosensors by integrating them with smartphones (Sivakumar and Lee, 2021; Kayani et al., 2023; Pu et al., 2024). With built-in high-definition cameras and RGB analysis apps, smartphones enable users to effortlessly capture and analyze fluorescence signals (Saadati, 2021; Kayani and Omer, 2022). Consequently, the combination of fluorescent chemosensors and smartphones is gaining attention as an efficient detection method that can rapidly and conveniently analyze analytes (Sáez-Hernández et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2022; Kathiravan et al., 2019; Mondal et al., 2020). Therefore, developing a FL chemosensor and its platform capable of quantitatively measuring Cu2+ through RGB analysis using a smartphone is crucial.

Herein, we have developed an effective fluorescent quantitative detection platform for Cu2+ and achieved on-site, convenient, and simplified detection for the first time, using NH2-H2BDC acid as a chemosensor probe, along with paper strip and a smartphone app as detectors. The probe exhibited highly fluorescent properties, making it excellent for analysis purposes. Additionally, we developed and applied a smartphone application named “Color Grab” as the detector for the visual detection method. This platform, consisting of fluorescent compounds (NH2-H2BDC), paper strip, and a smartphone, was utilized for the determination of Cu2+ in milk.

All chemicals utilized in this study were of analytical grade and were sourced from commercial suppliers without further purification. Specifically, NH2-H2BDC (99 %), Cu(NO3)2·6H2O, Zn(NO3)2, and Mg(NO3)2 were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich, while other compounds were sourced internally from our research facility. Deionized (DI) water was consistently employed throughout the experimental procedures.

We utilized a Cary 60 Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) to capture the UV–vis absorption spectra. FL spectra were acquired using a Cary Eclipse FL spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The detection of the compound's functional groups was performed with a Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer, specifically the FTIR Nicolet iS50 model (Thermo Scientific, USA).

To evaluate the FL QY of NH2-H2BDC, fluorescein was used as a reference. The UV–vis and FL spectra of both the reference and the sample were recorded. The integrated FL intensity and absorbance were calculated to determine the quantum yield of the NH2-H2BDC using the following equation (Kayani et al., 2024; Fan et al., 2022)

In this context, the subscripts S and R refer to the parameters for NH2-H2BDC and the reference, respectively. 'A' denotes the absorbance value at the excitation wavelength, 'I' represents the area under the FL emission spectra, and 'η' signifies the refractive index of the solvent used.

For a practical test, 150 mg of NH2-H2BDC powder was dissolved in 150 mL of DI water, followed by the addition of various concentrations of Cu2+ ranging from 230 to 330 nM. FL spectra were measured while the standard solutions were excited at 330 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone integrated with test strips application for detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The test paper sensor was fabricated as reported (Gil et al., 2024), following the steps below with minor modifications. Test paper strips, each sized 1 × 2 cm, were prepared using Ashless Whatman filter paper. These strips were then dipped in a 10 μM solution of fluorophore and dried in air. The fluorophore-containing test strips were subsequently dipped into standard solutions of Cu2+ and dried in air. Fluorescent images of the test strips were obtained using a Xiaomi Mi brand smartphone under a 365 nm UV lamp. The RGB values of the images were analyzed using the Color Grab app, a freely available mobile application.

Milk samples were sourced from a local supermarket. The preparation method followed established literature with minor adjustments (Singh et al., 2006). Specifically, 3 g of milk powder were ashed at around 450°C in a crucible for 2 hours. Then, 3 mL of concentrated HNO3 was added, and the mixture was heated to dissolve the residue. The resulting solution was diluted with DI water in a 50 mL volumetric flask, centrifuged at 4000 rpm (1073 × g) for 10 minutes, and filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane. This final solution was used to determine the copper content using the proposed sensor

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding mechanism of NH2-H2BDC toward Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectral studies to investigate the binding mode between the probe and Cu2+, As shown in Fig. 1 A, The NH2-H2BDC has several absorption peaks. First, the peaks at 3513 and 3396 cm−1 are attributed to NH2. These peaks do not appear when 2-aminoterephthalic acid reacts with Cu2+ ions, proving the involvement of the –NH2 group in the complex formation. The peak at 1685 cm−1 is attributed to the carbonyl groups of the NH2-H2BDC, and this peak also disappears when the NH2-H2BDC reacts with Cu2+ ions, indicating the involvement of the carboxylic group in covalent bonding to the copper ion. The (O–H) stretching vibrations do not appear in the spectra of NH2-H2BDC but do appear in the spectra of the complex as broad peaks at 3100 cm−1 and 3360 cm−1, respectively, suggesting the presence of water in the compound (Stanila et al., 2007). As a result of FTIR studies, the peaks of the NH2-H2BDC (red line) at 3513, 3396, and 1685 cm−1 were weakened to varying degrees after combining with copper ions to form a complex (blue line). This indicates strong and effective coordination between the amino groups and Cu2+.

Further to the sensing mechanism, when NH2-H2BDC captures Cu2+, it forms stable metal complexes through ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT), resulting in significant FL quenching of NH2-H2BDC, as illustrated in Fig. 2 C. This quenching occurs because the excited electrons are transferred to the empty orbital of Cu2+, preventing the excited state electrons from returning to the ground state orbitals of NH2-H2BDC (Li et al., 2022). Furthermore, the UV–vis spectra of NH2-H2BDC before and after interaction with Cu2+ were recorded. The UV–vis spectra of NH2-H2BDC reveal characteristic peaks at 260 nm and 340 nm, indicating the presence of specific functional groups on the NH2-H2BDC surface. However, these peaks disappear after the addition of Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 1B.

As depicted in Scheme 1, the Cu2+ ions coordinate with the functional groups on the surface of the NH2-H2BDC, forming the NH2-H2BDC/Cu2+ complex. Upon photoexcitation, an electron transfer occurs from NH2-H2BDC to the positively charged Cu2+ ions, resulting in the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process (Kayani et al., 2024; Bhatt et al., 2020). In conclusion, the addition of Cu2+ results in significant quenching of both the UV–visible absorption and FL spectra of NH2-H2BDC. Therefore, the observed FL quenching is attributed to PET.
The possible sensing mechanism of NH2-H2BDC with Cu2+ is shown in Scheme 1.

The photophysical properties of the NH2-H2BDC were systematically examined using a spectrofluorometer and a UV-Vis spectrophotometer. The UV–vis spectrum, depicted in Fig. 1B, exhibited a broad absorption range from 250 to 400 nm. Specifically, absorption peaks were observed at 256 and 340 nm, corresponding to the π–π* transition associated with the aromatic ring, and to the n–π* transition, which involves the nitrogen and oxygen lone pair electrons, respectively (Fahmi et al., 2019). These peaks indicate the presence of specific functional groups on the fluorophore's surface. As shown in Fig. 1B, the absorption peak of the probe at 340 nm decreased after the addition of Cu2+. This phenomenon can be explained by the lone pair electrons of the N-donor from NH2 in the compound donating to form a complex with the Cu2+ ion (Song et al., 2021; Aussawaponpaisan et al., 2017).

The excitation and emission spectra were examined. Excitation spectra were recorded by observing the emission at 425 nm, revealing a prominent peak at 330 nm. When excited at 330 nm, the NH2-H2BDC exhibited blue emission at 425 nm. As shown in Fig. 2 A.
The presence of the NH2-H2BDC was visually confirmed. As shown in Fig. 2B, the NH2-H2BDC solution is colorless in daylight but changes to a blue color when exposed to UV light at 365 nm.
As shown in Fig. 2 C, the FL of the NH2-H2BDC is present without Cu2+ ions, but when exposed to Cu2+ ions, the FL of the NH2-H2BDC diminishes.

The FL patterns of the fluorophore solution were observed. We conducted excitation across a range of wavelengths, starting from 300 nm up to 380 nm with increments of 10 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 2D. Initially, there was a rise in the intensity of emission peaks when excited between 300 and 330 nm. However, after excitation at 330 nm, there was a gradual decline. These results indicate that the most effective emission occurred at 425 nm when excited at 330 nm.
The QY of the NH2-H2BDC was measured in an aqueous solution was 81 %, indicating its high FL and sensitivity when used as a fluorescent probe.

The impact of pH on the FL intensity of NH2-H2BDC toward Cu2+ was also investigated. As seen in Fig. 3 A, the intensity of NH2-H2BDC across a pH range of 2.0–11.0 was not altered in the absence of Cu2+. However, after the addition of Cu2+ to NH2-H2BDC, the emission intensity of NH2-H2BDC across a pH range of 2.0–11.0 was not altered, showing effective suitability for detecting Cu2+ in various samples.

Reversibility and reusability experiments using ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) were conducted to investigate the robustness of NH2-H2BDC with respect to its cyclic binding ability to Cu2+ (Fig. 3B). NH2-H2BDC was prepared as a 4 μM solution in DI water, then Cu2+ was introduced into this solution at 50 μM, followed by EDTA at 50 μM. Successive cycles were carried out by adding more Cu2+, followed by EDTA. The addition of EDTA induced the appearance of an emission band at 425 nm, while further addition of Cu2+ led to the subsequent quenching of this emission band. These observations indicated that the FL "ON-OFF-ON" switching process remained effective even after being repeated five times, confirming the reusability and reversibility of NH2-H2BDC.

The (IS) strength on the FL intensity of the NH2-H2BDC-Cu2+ system was examined. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the presence of NaCl had no effect on the FL intensity of the system. Therefore, to simplify the experimental procedure and reduce reagent consumption, NaCl was omitted from the system.

The effect of temperatures ranging from 30 to 70 °C on the FL intensity of the system was studied. As shown in Fig. 4B, temperatures below 45 °C do not significantly impact the system. However, above 45 °C, the FL intensity decreases, likely due to increased molecular collisions, which enhance the likelihood of interactions between excited molecules and solvent molecules, thereby reducing fluorescence intensity (Kayani and Abdullah, 2024). To ensure consistency and reproducibility of experimental data, all subsequent experiments were conducted at room temperature.

The incubation time was systematically investigated to achieve better reproducibility and to understand the aggregation kinetics of the probe. Various incubation times, ranging from 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 min, were evaluated for the optimal detection of Cu2+ ions. In this study, 50 μM of Cu2+ was added to multiple volumetric flasks containing NH2-H2BDC (4 μM) As shown in Fig. 5 A, the FL of the NH2-H2BDC remained unchanged with increasing incubation times of 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 min, indicating that the reaction rapidly reached completion. Consequently, an incubation time within this range was selected as the optimal duration for detecting Cu2+ ions.

The FL emission of the probe with and without the metal ion was recorded to identify selective and sensitive detection of metal ions by observing changes in FL emission intensity, as shown in Fig. 6. The selectivity of the probe was evaluated in a DI water solution (NH2-H2BDC 4 μM, pH = 6.9) with various metal ions such as Ni2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+, Cd2+, Pb2+, and Ca2+. Significant changes in FL emission were observed with these metal ions under the same concentrations (1 mM) and identical conditions, while others did not exhibit such changes. Among these metal ions, Cu2+ showed a greater intensity variation in the selectivity FL spectrum. This indicates effective quenching, demonstrating an "On-Off" behavior with Cu2+ ions through energy or electron transfer processes, ultimately leading to a reverse photo-induced electron transfer process (Manigandan et al., 2020). Additionally, the quenching behavior may be attributed to the unfilled d subshell and paramagnetic properties of Cu2+ ions. The interaction of the probe with Cu2+ ions is confirmed by the FTIR spectra, as shown in Fig. 1 A. Additionally, to investigate the effect of certain molecules on FL emission and examine all possible molecules available in real samples, the effects of potential interfering molecules such as tyrosine (Tyr), lactose (Lact), ascorbic acid (AA), glycine (Gly), uric acid (UA), citric acid (CA), fructose (Fruc), phenylalanine (Phy), leucine Leu), starch (ST), aspartic acid (Asp), glucose (Glu), and cysteine (Cys) were investigated at a concentration of 1 mM each. The signal with interfering molecules exhibited no difference compared to that with Cu2+ ions alone, as shown in Fig. 6.

To further assess the potential anti-interference capability of NH2-H2BDC in the process of Cu²⁺ sensing, FL spectra were initially recorded after adding 1 mM of Cu²⁺ ions to solutions containing NH2-H2BDC and different metal ions (1 mM). The results indicated that the addition of other metal ions to the NH2-H2BDC solution did not quench the FL intensity (Fig. 6). However, upon the addition of Cu²⁺, a decrease in FL intensity was observed in all recorded spectra (Fig. 5B). Thus, it was concluded that the presence of other metal ions did not significantly interfere with the detection of Cu²⁺ by NH2-H2BDC.

To conduct a quantitative analysis and establish calibration curves, various standard solutions of Cu2+ were examined. Under optimized conditions, a calibration plot was constructed by plotting FL intensity against concentrations of standard Cu2+. In Fig. 7 A, it can be observed that as the Cu2+ concentration increased from 230 nM to 330 nM, the FL intensity decreased, concurrently with the reduction of the FL emission peak at 425 nm. Fig. 7B illustrates the correlation between Cu2+ ion concentration and FL intensity in the range of 230–330 nM, as described by the equation: I425 = 362.38 – 1.0047 [Cu2+].
The correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.992. The LOD for Cu2+ ions is 16.25 nM (S/N = 3.3), and the LOQ is 49.23 nM (S/N = 10).
The NH2-H2BDC exhibits higher sensitivity compared to other probes used for the quantification of Cu2+ in food samples, as shown in Table 1.

To enhance the visualization of Cu2+ ion detection, a new intelligent real-time sensing system has been developed. It utilizes fluorescent test paper and RGB color analysis software to minimize reliance on large detection equipment and enhance portability (Wang et al., 2023).

The process involves collecting green and blue values of the test paper with different concentrations of Cu2+ using RGB analysis “Color Grab” App. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the test papers were immersed in different concentrations of Cu2+. The FL intensity of the test paper decreased gradually when the concentration of Cu2+ increased from 15 to 90 μM under UV light irradiation at 365 nm. Furthermore, the ratio of green to blue with varying concentrations was used to create a calibration curve, as shown in Fig. 8B. This calibration curve displays a good linear relationship (R² = 0.9837) between the G/B ratio and the concentration of Cu2+, with a LOD calculated to be 11.07 μM with LOQ to be 33.56 μM. Thus, the aforementioned results validate that our low-cost sensing system is highly viable for detecting Cu2+ in everyday life applications.

As a result, the proposed probe exhibits numerous advantages, establishing it as a superior method. The NH2-H2BDC demonstrated sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ as a nanosensor, with a very low LOD of 16.25 nM and a LOQ of 49.23 nM. Additionally, the integration of a smartphone with paper strips functions as a sensor for detecting Cu2+, offering a low-cost, convenient, portable, and eco-friendly method for Cu2+ analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of in-solution FL for Copper (II) detection in milk samples
ce:section.ce:para: The Copper (II) samples were collected from milk sources. The efficiency of the recommended assay was assessed using the spike recovery method. Recovery rates for Copper (II) in spiked milk samples ranged from 96.15 % to 102.63 %. These results suggest that NH2-H2BDC can be effectively used to measure Copper (II) in real samples, as detailed in Table 2.

The environmental suitability of an analytical procedure is becoming increasingly important during its development. To determine the eco-friendliness of the sample preparation process and the entire analytical methodology, three separate analytical metrics were utilized (Kayani et al., 2024; Shatery et al., 2024; Kayani et al., 2024). In this context, two different green evaluation metrics, AGREEprep and BAGI, are employed to assess the environmental friendliness of both the sample preparation and the overall analytical procedure.

The AGREEprep software is a recent enhancement of the previously established AGREE tool. It evaluates criteria based on ten environmental sampling concepts: sample preparation, hazardous substances, renewable resources, waste management, sampling size, sample yield, automation, energy usage, analytical equipment, and safety measures. The overall value of this tool is determined by considering these ten factors (Gamal et al., 2021).

The AGREEprep tool was employed to assess the two developed approaches. As shown in Fig. 9, both methods exhibit environmentally friendly characteristics, with the smartphone-based approach achieving a higher analytical greenness score (0.87) in comparison to the fluorimetry method (0.78). This advantage is attributed to factors such as reduced sample size, capability to analyze multiple samples, lower energy consumption, and miniaturization. These findings affirm the safety, environmental friendliness, and eco-benign nature of the developed methodologies.

The introduction of the Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI) presents a novel metric for evaluating the practicality of an analytical method. BAGI assesses ten critical attributes, including the type of analysis, simultaneous determination of analytes, sample analysis rate, reagents and materials utilized, necessary instrumentation, level of automation, sample preparation method, simultaneous sample treatment, preconcentration needs, and sample quantity (Manousi et al., 2023).

We employed the BAGI approach to assess the eco-friendliness of the proposed methods utilizing fluorescent materials. As depicted in Fig. 10, the pictograms for the two methods show BAGI scores of 80 and 72.5, respectively, with the smartphone-based method scoring higher than the fluorimetry method. Furthermore, both methods demonstrate robust applicability for copper ion analysis.

In summary, the compounds of NH2-H2BDC have been successfully developed for the detection of Cu2+. The compounds exhibit a “turn-off” FL response to Cu2+, with detection limits of 16.25 nM and 11.07 μM for fluorimetry and smartphone methods, respectively. Test strips and a smartphone app were designed for quick, low-cost, and easy visual determination of Cu (II). Furthermore, greenness assessment tools such as AGREEprep and BAGI were applied to evaluate the environmental impact. The results show greater environmental friendliness, opening a new door to monitoring Cu2+ ions in various fields.
